cues.x: Today we are diving into a book that might change something you do about twenty five thousand times a day without ever thinking about it.
The book is called Breath, The New Science of a Lost Art, and it was written by James Nestor.
Nestor is a journalist, not a doctor and not a guru, and that is actually part of what makes this book so good.

He came to the subject the way you might, as a skeptic, as a person who was tired and stuffed up and quietly falling apart, wondering whether the simple act of breathing could really be the thing standing between him and better health.

What he found sent him around the world, into ancient burial crypts, into sleep labs, into free diving competitions, and eventually into a Stanford University study where he volunteered to have his nose completely plugged shut for ten straight days.
We will get to that, because it is one of the most gripping opening experiments you will ever hear about, and it sets up everything else.
So let me start there, with the nose plugs, because it is a little bit horrifying and completely convincing.
Nestor and another man, a breathing researcher named Anders Olsson, agreed to become their own laboratory rats.
Under the supervision of scientists at Stanford, they had their nostrils completely sealed.
Not partially.
Not with a cold you can sniff through.
Fully blocked, with silicone plugs and tape, so that for ten days and ten nights the only way air could get into their bodies was through the mouth.
This is the way a huge number of modern people already breathe, especially at night, so the experiment was not some bizarre torture for its own sake.
It was designed to measure, precisely, what happens to a healthy human body when you take the nose offline and force everything through the mouth.
Before it started, the Stanford team measured everything about the two men.
Blood pressure, heart rate variability, blood chemistry, the carbon dioxide in their blood, their nasal inflammation, how many times they stopped breathing in their sleep, how loudly they snored.
They had a baseline.
Then they plugged the noses and watched.
What happened was fast and it was ugly.
Within the first day or two, Nestor's body started to deteriorate in ways he could feel.
His blood pressure climbed.
By the end of the experiment it had risen dramatically, moving him toward what would be considered hypertension, a genuine risk factor for heart disease and stroke.
His heart rate variability, which is a marker of how resilient and relaxed your nervous system is, fell apart, which meant his body was living in a low grade state of stress the whole time.
But the nights were the worst.
Nestor had never really been a snorer.
Within days of breathing only through his mouth, he was snoring for hours every single night, and the sleep tracking equipment recorded something even more alarming.
He developed sleep apnea.
His breathing was actually stopping, over and over again, dozens of times an hour, his oxygen dipping while his brain jolted him half awake to gasp.
He went from a normal sleeper to a textbook apnea patient in less than a week, simply by breathing through the wrong hole.
He woke up exhausted, foggy, irritable, congested, his mouth bone dry, feeling ten years older.
Olsson had the same collapse.
Two healthy men, wrecked by mouth breathing alone.
And then came the second half, the part that gives the whole book its hope.
They took the plugs out.
They went back to breathing through the nose, and on top of that they started deliberately practicing slow, gentle nasal breathing.
The turnaround was almost as fast as the decline.
The snoring dropped away.
The sleep apnea events plummeted.
The blood pressure began to fall back toward normal.
The carbon dioxide levels in the blood settled.
Within a matter of days, both men were sleeping deeply again, waking up clearer, their bodies climbing back out of the hole.
That single experiment is the spine of the whole book.
It shows, in a healthy person, in real numbers, that how you breathe is not a footnote to your health.
It is closer to the foundation.
The route the air takes into your body changes your blood pressure, your sleep, your nervous system, and how old you feel.
That is the thesis Nestor spends the rest of the book proving from every angle.
How you breathe matters enormously, and most of us are doing it badly.
That is maybe the most humbling idea in the whole book, and I want you to sit with it for a second.
Nestor argues that modern humans have quietly become the worst breathers in the animal kingdom.
Not one of the worst.
The worst.
Wild animals breathe beautifully and instinctively.
Babies breathe beautifully, low into the belly, in through the nose.
And then somewhere along the way, through our diets, our stress, our stuffy indoor air, our chairs, our screens, and our chronically clenched, hurried lives, we forget.
We become mouth breathers.
We become over breathers.

We breathe shallow and fast and high in the chest, gulping far more air than our bodies actually need, and we pay for it with a long list of modern ailments that we usually blame on everything except the thing that is right under our nose.
So the promise of the book is genuinely radical and genuinely encouraging.

A lot of the chronic misery people carry around, the poor sleep, the anxiety, the high blood pressure, the fatigue, the crowded teeth and clogged sinuses, can be genuinely improved by relearning something you already know how to do.
You just have to do it correctly again.
So let us build up the toolkit the way Nestor does, one lesson at a time, and the first lesson, the foundation everything else rests on, is beautifully simple.
Nose, not mouth.
Breathe through your nose.
If you take nothing else from this whole episode, take that.
The nose is not a backup airway.
It is not just a passive pipe that happens to sit above your mouth.
It is a sophisticated, hardworking organ, and Nestor spends real time explaining everything it does that your mouth simply cannot.
When you inhale through your nose, the air does not just slide straight in.

It swirls through a maze of coiled structures called turbinates, and along the way that air gets filtered, so that dust, pollen, particles, and a good number of pathogens get trapped in mucus and swept away instead of going down into your lungs.
The air gets warmed to close to body temperature so it does not shock your delicate lung tissue.
It gets humidified, moistened, so your airways do not dry out.
And here is a piece most people have never heard.
The nose actually adds resistance and pressurizes the air.
That extra bit of back pressure means your lungs have more time and more pull to actually extract the oxygen out of each breath.
Nose breathing lets you pull significantly more oxygen out of the same air than mouth breathing does.
You literally get more from less.
And then there is the part that really lit Nestor up, and it turns out to be one of the most important molecules in the whole story.
Nitric oxide.
Deep in the sinuses, your nose is constantly producing a gas called nitric oxide, and this stuff is a small miracle.

It is a vasodilator, meaning it widens your blood vessels and airways, which improves circulation and lowers blood pressure and helps oxygen get absorbed and delivered throughout your body more efficiently.
On top of that, nitric oxide is antimicrobial.
It actually helps kill viruses, bacteria, and other pathogens as the air passes through.
Your nose is running a defense system and a circulation booster at the same time, for free, every time you inhale through it.
But here is the catch that makes it matter so much.
You only get that nitric oxide when you breathe through your nose.
Humming through your nose actually increases it even more.
The moment you switch to mouth breathing, you skip the whole system.
No filtering, no proper warming, no humidifying, no pressurizing, and crucially, no nitric oxide.
You are breathing on hard mode and getting less out of it.
So it should not surprise you that Nestor ties chronic mouth breathing to a grim list of problems.
Snoring, because the air is rushing over slack tissues in the throat.
Sleep apnea, that dangerous stop start breathing at night that starves your brain of oxygen and hammers your heart.
Dry mouth, which leads to more cavities and gum disease and bad breath, because saliva, which protects your teeth, evaporates when your mouth hangs open all night.
He connects it to worse sleep, more fatigue, higher blood pressure, and a body stuck in a stressed, sympathetic state.
Mouth breathing, especially the chronic nighttime kind that millions of people do without knowing it, is quietly corrosive.
The good news, the theme he keeps returning to, is that closing your mouth and reopening your nose reverses a lot of it.
Simple, free, and available to you tonight.
Now, if the nose is so obviously superior, you might ask the obvious question.
Why are so many of us stuck breathing through our mouths in the first place?
Why are our noses so congested, our airways so narrow, our sleep so broken?
And this is where Nestor takes a genuinely fascinating detour into the past, into the skulls.
He works with researchers who study human skulls, ancient ones and modern ones, and what they find is startling and consistent.
When you look at the skulls of ancient humans, our ancestors from before the industrial age, you see something almost strange to modern eyes.
You see broad, forward, well developed faces.
You see wide jaws and wide nasal passages and roomy airways.
And you see teeth.
Straight teeth.
Rows of them, perfectly aligned, with no crowding and no need for anyone to yank out wisdom teeth, because there was simply enough room in the jaw for every tooth to sit where it belonged.
These people, who never saw a dentist or an orthodontist, had the kind of mouths we now pay thousands of dollars trying to build with braces.
Then you look at modern industrialized human skulls, our skulls, and the picture flips.
Our faces have grown narrower and our jaws smaller and set further back.
Our mouths are too small for our teeth, which is why crowding, crookedness, and impacted wisdom teeth are practically universal now.
And when your jaw shrinks and your mouth crowds, something else shrinks with it.
Your airway.

A smaller, more recessed jaw means a narrower passage for air at the back of the throat, which means more obstruction, more snoring, more sleep apnea, and more of that fallback to mouth breathing because the nasal route feels too tight.
This change did not happen through slow evolution over hundreds of thousands of years.
It happened fast, over just a few hundred years, tracking almost perfectly with the industrial revolution and the arrival of modern processed food.
That speed is the clue.
It tells you the cause is not in our genes.
It is in our environment, in something we do or stopped doing.
And the main culprit Nestor lands on is chewing, or rather, the loss of it.
Think about what our ancestors ate.
Tough, raw, fibrous, hard food that required serious, sustained chewing, hour after hour, day after day, from early childhood.
Chewing is not just about breaking down food.
It is exercise for your face.
The jaw is built around bone and muscle that respond to stress the same way the rest of your skeleton does, by growing stronger and bigger when they are worked.
All that hard chewing during the years a child's face is developing sends a signal to build a big, wide, strong jaw with plenty of room.
Now look at the modern diet.
Soft.
Processed.
Cooked to mush, blended, ground, refined.
Smoothies and white bread and yogurt and food that practically dissolves in your mouth.
A modern child barely has to chew at all.
The signal to build a robust jaw never gets sent, so the jaw comes in small and underdeveloped, the teeth crowd for lack of space, and the airway closes down.
We softened our food and, over generations, we shrank our own faces and sabotaged our own breathing.
This opens up a thread in the book about oral posture, and about the strange world of people trying to reverse this in themselves and their kids.
The experts Nestor talks to emphasize something called proper oral posture, and it is worth memorizing because you can start doing it right now, sitting where you are.
Lips gently closed.
Teeth lightly touching or nearly so.
And here is the key part, your tongue resting up on the roof of your mouth, the whole tongue, not just the tip, pressed gently against the palate.
It turns out the tongue is meant to be a natural support structure for the upper jaw and the airway.
When your tongue lives on the roof of your mouth all day, it helps hold the whole architecture of your face wide and forward and open.
When it lies slack on the floor of the mouth, which is what happens when you mouth breathe, that support disappears and everything narrows.
This is the idea that has spun off into the online world under the name mewing, but the underlying science is old and real.
Keep your mouth closed, keep your tongue up, chew hard foods, and you are working with the design instead of against it.

Now we come to what I think is the single most counterintuitive, mind bending idea in the whole book, and if you have ever been told to take a big deep breath to calm down, this is going to rearrange your assumptions.
Nestor argues that most of us should breathe less, not more.
Less air.
Fewer breaths.
Smaller breaths.
Everything in our culture pushes the opposite.
Breathe big, fill your lungs, more oxygen is better.
But Nestor says that chronic over breathing, taking in far more air than your body needs, is actually making people sick, anxious, and tired.
And to understand why, you have to rethink a gas you have spent your whole life being told is just waste.
Carbon dioxide.
We are taught, more or less, that oxygen is the good gas we want and carbon dioxide is the garbage gas we need to get rid of.
Breathe in the good stuff, breathe out the bad stuff.
But that story is dangerously incomplete.
Carbon dioxide is not merely waste.
It is essential, and it plays a starring role in whether your body can actually use the oxygen you breathe in.
Here is the key science, and it has a name, the Bohr effect, discovered over a century ago.
Oxygen rides through your blood attached to hemoglobin in your red blood cells.
But for that oxygen to be released, to actually let go of the hemoglobin and cross into your muscles, your brain, your organs, the tissues that need it, there has to be enough carbon dioxide present.
Carbon dioxide is the signal that tells the blood, release the oxygen here, this is where it is needed.
So carbon dioxide is not the opposite of oxygen delivery.
It is the key that unlocks it.
Now watch what happens when you over breathe.

When you breathe too much, too fast, too hard, huffing and puffing beyond what your body actually requires, you do not load up on extra oxygen, because your blood is already almost fully saturated just from normal quiet breathing.
You cannot cram much more in.
What you do instead is blow off too much carbon dioxide.
You exhale away the very gas your body needs to release oxygen into your tissues.
So paradoxically, the harder and more you breathe, the less oxygen actually reaches your cells.
This is the cruel trick of chronic hyperventilation.
Your blood is full of oxygen, but your tissues are starved, because without enough carbon dioxide the oxygen will not come off the hemoglobin.
Think of a person having a panic attack, gasping and breathing wildly, and yet feeling dizzy, tingling, faint, like they cannot get enough air.
They are drowning in oxygen and starving for it at the same time, precisely because they have blown off their carbon dioxide.
Nestor's argument is that a milder, chronic version of this is happening to a huge slice of modern people all day long.
We over breathe our way into fatigue, brain fog, and anxiety.
The fix is to build up your tolerance to carbon dioxide, to teach your body to be comfortable with a little more of it, and you do that by breathing less.
By slowing down.
By taking smaller, calmer, gentler breaths, and by extending your exhales.
Over time your body resets its chemistry, becomes more efficient, and delivers oxygen better with less effort.
This is the deep logic underneath so many of the breathing traditions the book explores.
Less is more.
And when you retrain yourself this way, people report calmer nerves, steadier energy, and better endurance, because the whole system is finally running the way it was designed to.
Which brings us to what might be the most practical, do it tonight gem in the entire book.
The perfect breath.
Researchers, working with all kinds of people and all kinds of instruments, kept converging on a single rhythm that seems to be a sweet spot for the human body.
Roughly five and a half seconds to breathe in, and roughly five and a half seconds to breathe out.
That works out to about five and a half breaths per minute.
Slow.
Much slower than most of us breathe when we are not paying attention, which is often more like fifteen or twenty times a minute.
When you settle into that rhythm, about five and a half breaths a minute, something remarkable happens in your body.
You land near what scientists call the resonant frequency, and at that frequency the systems of your body fall into a kind of harmony.
Your heart rate, your blood pressure, your circulation, and your nervous system start to sync up and pulse together in a coherent, efficient wave.
Your heart rate variability, that marker of calm and resilience, shoots up.
Your blood pressure eases.
Your body tips out of stress mode and into rest and recovery mode.
It is, in a real physical sense, the tempo your body wants to run at.
And here is the detail that gave Nestor chills, and gives me chills too.
This rhythm is not new.
Humans have stumbled onto it over and over for thousands of years, hidden inside prayer and ritual.

When researchers measured people reciting the traditional Latin rosary, the pattern of Hail Marys and the cycles of the beads, they found that it slowed the breath to almost exactly five and a half breaths per minute.
When they measured Buddhist monks chanting the om mani padme hum mantra, same thing, right around that magic rhythm.

Yoga breathing, certain forms of chanting, the cadence of various sacred recitations across cultures that never spoke to one another, all of them, when you time the breath, land in that same slow zone of roughly six breaths a minute.
Nestor's point is beautiful.

These practices survived for millennia and spread across the world partly because they made people feel calm, centered, and well, and the reason they made people feel that way, whether anyone understood the physiology or not, is that they were quietly tuning the breath to the body's resonant frequency.
Prayer and meditation have been slow breathing exercises in disguise the whole time.
The perfect breath was hiding inside human culture, waiting for a heart rate monitor to reveal it.
So put those threads together and you have most of the practical core of the book.
Breathe through your nose to get the filtering and the nitric oxide and the pressurized, efficient oxygen exchange.
Breathe less, gently, to keep enough carbon dioxide in your system so your tissues actually receive that oxygen.
And breathe slow, around five and a half breaths a minute, to drop into resonance and calm your whole nervous system.
Nose.
Less.
Slow.
These simple levers, Nestor documents, can measurably lower blood pressure, sometimes as effectively as some medications for people with mild hypertension.

They can improve athletic performance and endurance, which is why more and more elite athletes train with their mouths taped shut or their breathing deliberately restricted, forcing nasal breathing and carbon dioxide tolerance so their bodies become more efficient.
And they can powerfully calm anxiety, because a slow exhale is a direct physical off switch for the fight or flight response.
Your breath is the one part of your autonomic nervous system you can consciously grab hold of, and through it you can reach in and steady your heart and your mind.
Let me tell you about one more character, because his story is quietly one of the most moving in the book.
His name was Carl Stough, and he was a choir conductor, not a scientist, which makes what he accomplished all the more striking.
Stough became obsessed with something most of us never think about at all.
The exhale.
The out breath.
He developed an approach he called breathing coordination, and his core insight was that most people, even sick ones, are not actually failing to breathe in.
They are failing to breathe out.
They exhale weakly and incompletely, leaving stale, spent air trapped at the bottom of their lungs, so that every new breath only refreshes a fraction of the total.
If you never fully empty your lungs, you can never fully fill them, and you leave your body starved no matter how hard you gulp for air.
Stough started working with emphysema patients, people with badly damaged lungs who were often gasping, oxygen starved, sometimes bedridden and considered essentially untreatable.

By retraining these patients to fully engage the diaphragm and completely empty their lungs on the exhale, working the muscles of respiration back to life, he produced results that stunned the doctors watching.
Patients who had been failing began to breathe more freely, to move air they had not moved in years, some of them getting up and walking and living again.

And then, in one of those wonderful cross overs, Stough took the same method to opera singers and Olympic runners, elite performers who depend on their breath, and made them measurably better at their craft by teaching them to exhale completely and coordinate the whole apparatus of breathing.
The lesson Nestor pulls out of Carl Stough's life is one we tend to forget entirely.
A great breath begins with a great exhale.
Empty out fully, all the way, squeeze the old air out, and the fresh breath rushes in on its own.
Do not just think about breathing in.
Think about breathing all the way out.
Now, all of this raises a very real practical problem, and Nestor is honest about it, because he had it himself.
It is easy to promise yourself you will breathe through your nose.

It is much harder to keep your mouth shut for eight hours while you are unconscious and asleep, which is exactly when mouth breathing does the most damage, the snoring and the apnea and the dry mouth and the wrecked sleep.
So what is the trick?
Here Nestor introduces something that sounds a little absurd the first time you hear it, but that he swears by and that the airway experts he consults recommend.
Mouth taping.

At night, before bed, he places a small piece of tape gently over the center of his lips, just enough to keep his mouth from falling open in his sleep and to nudge him back to breathing through his nose all night long.
He is careful to describe it as a small, light piece of tape, not a gag, just a gentle reminder to the sleeping body.
And he reports that it transformed his sleep.
Less snoring, deeper rest, waking up with a moist mouth instead of a desert, feeling genuinely restored.
Many people who try it describe the same thing, that it quietly fixed a problem they did not fully realize they had.
It is a strange little hack, but it flows directly from the book's foundation.
If the nose is the right way to breathe, then the goal is to breathe through it even when you are not awake to choose.
So far everything we have talked about lives on the calming, gentle, less is more side of breathing.
Nose breathing, slow breathing, full exhales, breathing lightly.

But Nestor is careful to show you that there is a whole other, wilder side to this science, a set of methods that go in the opposite direction, using intense, heavy, deliberate breathing to crank the body up rather than settle it down.
And the poster child for this world is a man you may have heard of, a Dutchman named Wim Hof, sometimes called the Iceman.
Wim Hof has become semi legendary for doing things that seem physically impossible.
Sitting submerged in ice for hours.
Climbing to brutal altitudes on Everest in nothing but shorts.
Running barefoot across snow and ice, withstanding cold that should be dangerous, even deadly, for an ordinary human.
And his secret, the engine behind these feats, is a specific style of powerful, rhythmic breathing.

The Wim Hof method, in broad strokes, involves rounds of very heavy, deliberate over breathing, dozens of big full breaths in a row, deliberately flushing the system, followed by holding the breath on empty for as long as you comfortably can, and then repeating.

This intense pattern deliberately shifts the body's chemistry, floods it with oxygen, changes the balance of gases, and triggers a cascade of effects, a surge of adrenaline, a shift in blood pH, a flood of alertness and heat.

What makes Hof scientifically important, and not just a stunt man, is that researchers put him and his trained students in a laboratory and injected them with a bacterial toxin that normally makes any healthy person violently sick, feverish, shaking, miserable.
Using his breathing method, Hof and his students were able to consciously blunt that immune response, staying calmer and less sick than should have been possible.

They demonstrated, under scientific observation, that a human being can use breath to reach into systems long considered completely automatic and beyond conscious control, the immune system, the inflammatory response, the autonomic nervous system.
That was supposed to be impossible.
Breath made it possible.
This heavy, heat generating style of breathing is not entirely new either.
It echoes an ancient practice from the Tibetan Buddhist tradition called Tummo, sometimes translated as inner fire.

For centuries, advanced practitioners in the Himalayas have used forceful breathing techniques combined with intense visualization to generate so much internal heat that they can reportedly sit in freezing mountain conditions, even drying icy wet sheets draped over their bare bodies with the warmth their breath produces.

What was once considered a mystical, almost supernatural feat of monks turns out to have a real physiological basis in the way deliberate heavy breathing can alter body temperature, blood chemistry, and the nervous system.

Nestor also touches on related conscious breathing practices, including holotropic breathwork, a form of very fast, deep, sustained breathing developed to produce profound altered states of consciousness and emotional release.
These are the extreme end of the spectrum, the breathing plus methods, powerful and not to be done casually, but they prove the same core point from the opposite direction.
Your breath is a control panel for your body and mind, and it reaches far deeper than anyone assumed.

Now, sitting between the wild heavy methods and the everyday gentle ones is a school of breathing that has become one of the most important in the whole field, and it deserves its own moment because it treats real disease.
It is called the Buteyko method, named after the Ukrainian doctor who developed it, Konstantin Buteyko.
Working in the mid twentieth century, Buteyko became convinced that a great many chronic conditions, and especially asthma, were being made worse, or even caused, by chronic over breathing.
That same villain again, taking in too much air, blowing off too much carbon dioxide, keeping the body in a state of imbalance.

Buteyko's insight was that many asthmatics, and many anxious and unwell people generally, are habitual over breathers, and that their gasping, heaving, mouth open breathing is not just a symptom of their distress but a driver of it.
His method, in essence, is training people to breathe less.

To breathe lightly, gently, quietly, always through the nose, to reduce the total volume of air moving in and out, and to gradually build up tolerance to carbon dioxide, so the airways can relax and the whole system can normalize.

For asthma sufferers, learning to breathe less and to breathe through the nose has, in many cases, dramatically reduced their symptoms and their reliance on rescue inhalers, though of course anyone with a serious medical condition should work with their doctor and not abandon their medicine.

The Buteyko method is really the clinical, therapeutic embodiment of the book's central counterintuitive lesson, that for most of us the path to health is not more air but less, taken slowly and calmly through the nose.
It closes the loop back to the carbon dioxide science and the Bohr effect and the perfect breath.
Breathe less, breathe through your nose, and let your body rediscover its balance.
So step back and take in the whole landscape Nestor has drawn.
On one side you have the slow, calming, restorative methods.
Nasal breathing, the perfect breath at five and a half a minute, full deep exhales, the light quiet breathing of Buteyko, the ancient rhythms of prayer and mantra.
These down regulate you, calm your nerves, lower your blood pressure, and restore your sleep.
On the other side you have the intense, stimulating, energizing methods.

Wim Hof, Tummo, holotropic breathing, the heavy conscious over breathing that fires up the body, generates heat, floods the system, and lets people push into immune and nervous system territory once thought untouchable.
And the deep message of the book is that both of these are available to you.
Breath is not one thing.

It is a dial you can turn in either direction, toward calm or toward activation, toward rest or toward power, all with nothing but the muscles you were born with and the air that is already all around you.
The tragedy Nestor documents is that most modern people never touch the dial at all.
They breathe badly, through the mouth, too fast, too shallow, all day and all night, and never realize there is a lever right there under their nose.
And that is really the hopeful heart of this book, the reason it has landed so hard with so many people.
Breathing is a free, always available, always with you lever on your own health.
You do not need a prescription.
You do not need a gym membership or a supplement or a device.
You do not need money or a specialist.
You carry the tool with you into every moment of your life, awake and asleep, and you have been carrying it your whole life without being taught how to use it.
The science Nestor gathers, some of it cutting edge, much of it rediscovered from traditions thousands of years old, all points to the same handful of simple, powerful, doable changes.
And the beauty is that they cost nothing and you can start today.
So let me leave you with something concrete, a plan for this week, because a book like this is worthless if it just becomes one more interesting thing you heard and forgot.
Let us make it real.
Here is what you can actually do, starting now, in the car or at the gym or wherever you are.
First, and above all, close your mouth.
Make nasal breathing your default.
Right now, as you listen, notice how you are breathing.
If your mouth is open, close it gently, rest your tongue on the roof of your mouth, and breathe in and out through your nose.
Do it during your commute, during your workout, on your walks.
If your nose feels too blocked to breathe through, that congestion often eases within days once you stop mouth breathing, so give it time and keep gently insisting on the nose.
This one change, all by itself, is the single most important thing in the entire book.
Second, tonight, consider trying to protect your nose breathing while you sleep, because that is when it matters most and when you have the least control.
If you snore, or wake up with a dry mouth, or wake up tired no matter how long you slept, this is likely your biggest opportunity.
Some people, following Nestor, use a small, gentle piece of tape over the lips at night to keep the mouth from falling open.
If that feels like too much, start by simply being mindful as you fall asleep, mouth closed, breathing softly through the nose.
Prioritize this, because good breathing at night quietly rebuilds your health while you are unconscious.
Third, practice the perfect breath.

Once a day, set aside even five or ten minutes, sitting comfortably, and slow your breathing down to roughly five and a half seconds in through the nose and five and a half seconds out through the nose.
About six breaths a minute.
You do not need to count perfectly, just slow, smooth, and gentle, in and out, low into the belly.
Do it in the morning to set a calm tone, or in the evening to unwind, or in any moment you feel stress rising.
This is your direct line to your nervous system, your built in off switch for anxiety.
When you feel panic or anger or overwhelm creeping in, remember, slow the breath, lengthen the exhale, and your body will follow your breath into calm.
Fourth, remember the exhale.
Carl Stough's great lesson.
Do not just chase the inhale.

When you breathe, especially during your slow breathing practice, focus on breathing all the way out, emptying your lungs completely and gently, letting the belly draw in at the end, squeezing out the stale air.
Then the fresh breath will come in on its own.
A full exhale is the foundation of a full breath.
Fifth, breathe less, not more.
Let go of the idea that big, heavy, mouth gulping breaths are healthy.
Aim for light, quiet, gentle breathing through the nose most of the time.
If you catch yourself sighing, yawning, gasping, or breathing hard when you are just sitting still, gently rein it in and soften it.
Comfort with a little more carbon dioxide is a sign of a healthy, efficient, calm body.
And sixth, over the longer term, respect your jaw and your chewing.
Give your mouth real work to do.
Eat harder, tougher, less processed foods that make you chew, actually chew, instead of everything soft and blended and pre broken down.
And throughout the day, hold that good oral posture, lips together, teeth lightly touching, tongue resting up on the roof of your mouth.
It sounds tiny, but it is the daily maintenance of the very architecture that lets you breathe well in the first place.
Nose, not mouth.
Exhale fully.
Slow it down to about six breaths a minute.
And most of the time, breathe less, not more.
That is the whole art, lost and now found again, distilled into a few rules you can carry for the rest of your life.

James Nestor's Breath is ultimately a book about reclaiming something that was stolen from us slowly, over generations, by soft food and stress and bad habits and a culture that told us to breathe big when we should have been breathing well.
The oldest, simplest, most constant act of your life turns out to be one of the most powerful levers you have over your own health, your sleep, your energy, your anxiety, and even how you age.
And the best part, the part worth holding onto as you go about your day, is that you already have everything you need.
The next breath is right there.
So take it slow, take it through your nose, let it out all the way, and begin.
